title: Live AI Q&A + Crushing it in Chess at the Same Time - Come Hang Out!
summary: Cole Medin is hosting a live AI Q&A session on Saturday where viewers can ask questions about AI coding, agents, and Claude Code. The stream will also feature chess gameplay alongside the discussion, with regular content drops on YouTube.
writeup: A casual live-stream Q&A covering agentic AI coding workflows and model-tier selection, streamed from a hotel room while the host plays blitz chess. The substantive thread is benchmarking results from a community workshop: for a four-stage agentic pipeline (explore, plan, implement, validate), reserving a frontier model only for the planning stage and using a cheaper model for the rest nearly matches full-frontier quality at a fraction of the token cost. The discussion also touches on Anthropic's productivity claims for Claude and the emerging landscape of cheaper capable models.
key_points: Benchmarking across an explore-plan-implement-validate agentic coding workflow shows that using Opus (or an equivalent frontier model) only for the planning stage, then handing a strong spec to Kimi K2.6 for exploration, implementation, and validation, produces results close to using a frontier model throughout, while saving hundreds of thousands of tokens from the most expensive model.

Minimax M3, released the prior week, is roughly one-quarter the price of Kimi K2.6 and performs well enough for trivial and routine agentic tasks, making it a candidate for the non-planning stages when cost is the binding constraint.

Anthropic's blog reported 8x lines of code per engineer per quarter in Q2 2026 using Claude; the host flags that lines of code is a quantity measure not a quality measure, and the true productivity gain is almost certainly lower than 8x.

The four-stage breakdown (explore, plan, implement, validate) is described as a near-universal structure across agentic engineering processes, whether using a custom workflow, BMAD, GSD, or another framework.

chapters.title: Four-Stage Pipeline: Explore, Plan, Implement, Validate
Kimi K2.6 as the Cheaper Tier
Minimax M3 and Routine Task Models
